revision.comment: 新しいページ：倭隋（古墳時代の人物）。
revision.text:
'''倭 隋'''（わ ずい、[[生没年不詳]]）は、[[5世紀]]前半（[[古墳時代]]中期）の[[倭]]の人物。[[珍|倭王珍]]（[[倭の五王]]の1人）の臣。
== 記録 == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} 中国歴史書の『[[宋書]]』[[文帝 (南朝宋)|文帝]]紀 [[元嘉]]15年（[[438年]]）己巳条および夷蛮伝倭国の条（宋書倭国伝）によれば、珍が[[宋 (南朝)|宋]]に遣使貢献して「安東将軍 倭国王」に除正された際、珍は倭隋ら13人に対する平西・征虜・冠軍・輔国将軍号の除正も求め、文帝は詔して全て認めたという<ref name="東洋文庫 宋書">『東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（[[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]264）』 平凡社、1974年、pp. 309-313。</ref><ref name="講談社 宋書">『倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（[[講談社学術文庫]]2010）』 講談社、2010年、pp. 117-123。</ref>。
== 考証 == 倭隋の詳細は明らかでないが、倭王と共通する「倭」姓であるため、ヤマト王権内では王族将軍が支持基盤であった様子が示唆される<ref>坂元義種 「倭の五王」『国史大辞典』 吉川弘文館。</ref>。ただし平西将軍は安東将軍と同じ第三品で、品内でも1階しか違わない点が注意される<ref name="森公章"/>{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。このことから、当時の倭では王と同程度の人物が補佐する統治構造であったとする説があり<ref name="森公章">[[森公章]] 『倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）』 山川出版社、2010年、pp. 47-50。</ref>、その様子と[[百舌鳥古墳群]]・[[古市古墳群]]の並立との対応をみる説もある{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
また、高句麗王・百済王・倭王の場合は「征東・鎮東・安東」など中国の視点の将軍号であるが、倭隋の場合は「平西」という倭の視点の将軍号になる点についても注意される<ref name="森公章"/>{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。これを、安「東」将軍と平「西」将軍の二大巨頭の下に征虜・冠軍・輔国将軍を置いた統治機構と見なす説もある{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
そのほか、倭の五王においては珍・済の続柄が明らかでないが、珍・済が別々の勢力（百舌鳥古墳群・古市古墳群）であるとして、済は倭隋の系統とする説がある{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
なお、倭の五王の一連の記事において倭王以外で名称の記載があるのは、倭隋のほか[[讃|倭王讃]]の臣の[[曹達 (倭)|司馬曹達]]のみになる。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=河内春人|year=2018|chapter=|title=倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-（[[中公新書]]2470）|publisher=[[中央公論新社]]|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|河内春人|2018}}}}
== 関連項目 == * [[倭の五王]] * [[珍]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://hanchi.ihp.sinica.edu.tw/ihp/hanji.htm 漢籍電子文献資料庫] - 台湾中央研究院
{{デフォルトソート:わ すい}} [[Category:古墳時代の人物]] [[Category:魏晋南北朝時代の人物]] [[Category:倭人伝]]

そのほか、倭の五王においては珍・済の続柄が明らかでないが、珍・済が別々の勢力（百舌鳥古墳群・古市古墳群）であるとして、済は倭隋の系統とする説がある{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
なお、倭の五王の一連の記事において倭王以外で名称の記載があるのは、倭隋のほか[[讃|倭王讃]]の臣の[[曹達 (倭)|司馬曹達]]のみになる。

{{デフォルトソート:わ すい}} [[Category:古墳時代の人物]] [[Category:魏晋南北朝時代の人物]] [[Category:倭人伝]]

'''倭 隋'''（わ ずい、[[生没年不詳]]）は、[[5世紀]]前半（[[古墳時代]]中期）の[[倭]]の人物。[[珍|倭王珍]]（[[倭の五王]]の1人）の臣。
== 記録 == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} 中国歴史書の『[[宋書]]』[[文帝 (南朝宋)|文帝]]紀 [[元嘉 (南朝宋)|元嘉]]15年（[[438年]]）己巳条および夷蛮伝倭国の条（宋書倭国伝）や『[[南史]]』「巻69「列伝79」夷貊下東夷伝倭国条[https://zh.wikisource.org/wiki/南史/卷79#倭國]によれば、珍が[[宋 (南朝)|宋]]に遣使貢献して「安東将軍 倭国王」に除正された際、珍は倭隋ら13人に対する平西・征虜・冠軍・輔国将軍号の除正も求め、文帝は詔して全て認めたという<ref name="東洋文庫 宋書">『東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（[[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]264）』 平凡社、1974年、pp. 309-313。</ref><ref name="講談社 宋書">『倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（[[講談社学術文庫]]2010）』 講談社、2010年、pp. 117-123。</ref>。
== 考証 == 倭隋の詳細は明らかでないが、倭王と共通する「倭」姓であるため、ヤマト王権内では王族将軍が支持基盤であった様子が示唆される<ref>坂元義種 「倭の五王」『国史大辞典』 吉川弘文館。</ref>。ただし平西将軍は安東将軍と同じ第三品で、品内でも1階しか違わない点が注意される<ref name="森公章"/>{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。このことから、当時の倭では[[卑弥呼]]と男弟の関係のように王と同程度の人物が補佐する統治構造であったとする説があり<ref name="森公章">[[森公章]] 『倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）』 山川出版社、2010年、pp. 47-50。</ref>、その様子と[[百舌鳥古墳群]]・[[古市古墳群]]或いは[[佐紀盾列古墳群]]の並立との対応をみる説もある{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
また、高句麗王・百済王・倭王の場合は「征東・鎮東・安東」など中国の視点の将軍号であるが、倭隋の場合は「平西」という倭の視点の将軍号になる点についても注意される<ref name="森公章"/>{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。これを、安「東」将軍と平「西」将軍の二大巨頭の下に征虜・冠軍・輔国将軍を置いた統治機構と見なす説もある{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
そのほか、[[倭の五王]]においては珍・済の続柄が明らかでないが、珍・済が別々の勢力（百舌鳥古墳群・古市古墳群）であるとして、済は倭隋の系統とする説がある{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
『[[古事記]]』分注の年代により珍を[[允恭天皇]]とすると『[[日本書紀]]』即位前紀冒頭に出てくる[[大草香皇子]]（異母弟で日向髪長媛の子）が倭隋の候補になる。
なお、[[倭の五王]]の一連の記事において倭王以外で名称の記載があるのは、倭隋のほか[[讃|倭王讃]]の臣の[[曹達 (倭)|司馬曹達]]のみになる。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=河内春人|year=2018|chapter=|title=倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-（[[中公新書]]2470）|publisher=[[中央公論新社]]|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|河内春人|2018}}}}
== 関連項目 == * [[倭の五王]] * [[珍]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://hanchi.ihp.sinica.edu.tw/ihp/hanji.htm 漢籍電子文献資料庫] - 台湾中央研究院
{{デフォルトソート:わ すい}} [[Category:古墳時代の人物]] [[Category:魏晋南北朝時代の人物]] [[Category:倭人伝]]

また、高句麗王・百済王・倭王の場合は「征東・鎮東・安東」など中国の視点の将軍号であるが、倭隋の場合は「平西」という倭の視点の将軍号になる点についても注意される<ref name="森公章"/>{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。これを、安「東」将軍と平「西」将軍の二大巨頭の下に征虜・冠軍・輔国将軍を置いた統治機構と見なす説もある{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
そのほか、[[倭の五王]]においては珍・済を別人と考える場合、続柄が明らかでないが、珍・済が別々の勢力（百舌鳥古墳群・古市古墳群）であるとして、済は倭隋の系統とする説がある{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。

'''倭 隋'''（わ ずい、[[生没年不詳]]）は、[[5世紀]]前半（[[古墳時代]]中期）の[[倭]]の人物。[[珍|倭王珍]]（[[倭の五王]]の1人）の臣。
== 記録 == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} 中国歴史書の『[[宋書]]』[[文帝 (南朝宋)|文帝]]紀 [[元嘉 (南朝宋)|元嘉]]15年（[[438年]]）己巳条および夷蛮伝倭国の条（宋書倭国伝）や『[[南史]]』「巻69「列伝79」夷貊下東夷伝倭国条[https://zh.wikisource.org/wiki/南史/卷79#倭國]によれば、珍が[[宋 (南朝)|宋]]に遣使貢献して「安東将軍 倭国王」に除正された際、珍は倭隋ら13人に対する平西・征虜・冠軍・輔国将軍号の除正も求め、文帝は詔して全て認めたという<ref name="東洋文庫 宋書">『東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（[[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]264）』 平凡社、1974年、pp. 309-313。</ref><ref name="講談社 宋書">『倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（[[講談社学術文庫]]2010）』 講談社、2010年、pp. 117-123。</ref>。
== 考証 == 倭隋の詳細は明らかでないが、倭王と共通する「倭」姓であるため、ヤマト王権内では王族将軍が支持基盤であった様子が示唆される<ref>坂元義種 「倭の五王」『国史大辞典』 吉川弘文館。</ref>。ただし平西将軍は安東将軍と同じ第三品で、品内でも1階しか違わない点が注意される<ref name="森公章"/>{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。このことから、当時の倭では[[卑弥呼]]と男弟の関係のように王と同程度の人物が補佐する統治構造であったとする説があり<ref name="森公章">[[森公章]] 『倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）』 山川出版社、2010年、pp. 47-50。</ref>、その様子と[[百舌鳥古墳群]]・[[古市古墳群]]或いは[[佐紀盾列古墳群]]の並立との対応をみる説もある{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
また、高句麗王・百済王・倭王の場合は「征東・鎮東・安東」など中国の視点の将軍号であるが、倭隋の場合は「平西」という倭の視点の将軍号になる点についても注意される<ref name="森公章"/>{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。これを、安「東」将軍と平「西」将軍の二大巨頭の下に征虜・冠軍・輔国将軍を置いた統治機構と見なす説もある{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
そのほか、[[倭の五王]]においては珍・済を別人と考える場合、続柄が明らかでないが、珍・済が別々の勢力（百舌鳥古墳群・古市古墳群）であるとして、済は倭隋の系統とする説がある{{Sfn|河内春人|2018|pp=73-119}}。
なお、[[倭の五王]]の一連の記事において倭王以外で名称の記載があるのは、倭隋のほか[[讃|倭王讃]]の臣の[[曹達 (倭)|司馬曹達]]のみになる。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}

revision.text:
{{Cite sources clearly|date=September 2015}} '''Wa Zui''' (倭 隋, {{ill|Lifespan Unknown|ja|生没年不詳}}) was a figure from the early [[5th century]] (middle [[Kofun period]]) in [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]]. He served as a retainer of {{ill|Wa King Chin|ja|珍}}, one of the {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}.
== Records == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} According to Chinese historical texts such as the {{ill|Book of Song|ja|宋書}}, the {{ill|Wendi (Liu Song)|ja|文帝}} section, [[Yuanjia (Liu Song)|Yuanjia]] 15th year ({{ill|438|ja|438年}}) Jisi entry, and the section on foreign peoples in the {{ill|History of the Southern Dynasties|ja|南史}} (Chapter 69, "Eastern Barbarians of Wa")<ref name="Toyobunko">『{{ill|The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja|東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（東洋文庫264）}}』 Heibonsha, 1974, pp. 309-313.</ref><ref name="Kodansha">『{{ill|Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja|倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（講談社学術文庫2010）}}』 Kodansha, 2010, pp. 117-123.</ref>, Chin sent an envoy to the {{ill|Liu Song dynasty|ja|宋}} court, offering tributes and receiving the title of "General Pacifying the East and King of Wa." Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the {{ill|Yamato polity|ja|ヤマト王権}}.<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}. This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}} and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）}}』 Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2010, pp. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of burial mounds like the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}, {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Group|ja|古市古墳群}}, or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|Wa King San|ja|讃}}.
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Kawachi Haruhito|year=2018|title={{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja|倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-}} (Chūō Kōron Shinsho 2470)|publisher={{ill|Chūōkōron-shinsha|ja|中央公論新社}}|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|Kawachi Haruhito|2018}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}} * {{ill|Wa King Chin|ja|珍}}
== External Links == * [http://hanchi.ihp.sinica.edu.tw/ihp/hanji.htm Chinese Classics Electronic Database] - Academia Sinica, Taiwan
[[Category:Kofun period figures]] [[Category:Figures in Wa Chronicles]] [[Category:Date of birth unknown]] [[Category:Date of death unknown]] [[ja:倭隋]]

{{Cite sources clearly|date=September 2015}} '''Wa Zui''' (倭 隋, {{ill|Lifespan Unknown|ja|生没年不詳}}) was a figure from the early [[5th century]] (middle [[Kofun period]]) in [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]]. He served as a retainer of {{ill|Wa King Chin|ja|珍}}, one of the {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}.
== Records == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} According to Chinese historical texts such as the {{ill|Book of Song|ja|宋書}}, the {{ill|Wendi (Liu Song)|ja|文帝}} section, [[Yuanjia (Liu Song)|Yuanjia]] 15th year ({{ill|438|ja|438年}}) Jisi entry, and the section on foreign peoples in the {{ill|History of the Southern Dynasties|ja|南史}} (Chapter 69, "Eastern Barbarians of Wa")<ref name="Toyobunko">『{{ill|The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja|東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（東洋文庫264）}}』 Heibonsha, 1974, pp. 309-313.</ref><ref name="Kodansha">『{{ill|Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja|倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（講談社学術文庫2010）}}』 Kodansha, 2010, pp. 117-123.</ref>, Chin sent an envoy to the {{ill|Liu Song dynasty|ja|宋}} court, offering tributes and receiving the title of "General Pacifying the East and King of Wa." Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the {{ill|Yamato polity|ja|ヤマト王権}}.<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}. This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}} and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）}}』 Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2010, pp. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of burial mounds like the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}, {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Group|ja|古市古墳群}}, or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|Wa King San|ja|讃}}.
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Kawachi Haruhito|year=2018|title={{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja|倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-}} (Chūō Kōron Shinsho 2470)|publisher={{ill|Chūōkōron-shinsha|ja|中央公論新社}}|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|Kawachi Haruhito|2018}}}}

Regarding the {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|Wa King San|ja|讃}}.

{{Cite sources clearly|date=September 2015}} '''Wa Zui''' (倭 隋, {{ill|Lifespan Unknown|ja|生没年不詳}}) was a figure from the early [[5th century]] (middle [[Kofun period]]) in [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]]. He served as a retainer of {{ill|Wa King Chin|ja|珍}}, one of the {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}.
== Records == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} According to Chinese historical texts such as the {{ill|Book of Song|ja|宋書}}, the {{ill|Wendi (Liu Song)|ja|文帝}} section, [[Yuanjia (Liu Song)|Yuanjia]] 15th year ({{ill|438|ja|438年}}) Jisi entry, and the section on foreign peoples in the {{ill|History of the Southern Dynasties|ja|南史}} (Chapter 69, "Eastern Barbarians of Wa")<ref name="Toyobunko">『{{ill|The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja|東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（東洋文庫264）}}』 Heibonsha, 1974, pp. 309-313.</ref><ref name="Kodansha">『{{ill|Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja|倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（講談社学術文庫2010）}}』 Kodansha, 2010, pp. 117-123.</ref>, Chin sent an envoy to the {{ill|Liu Song dynasty|ja|宋}} court, offering tributes and receiving the title of "General Pacifying the East and King of Wa." Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the {{ill|Yamato polity|ja|ヤマト王権}}.<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}. This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}} and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）}}』 Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2010, pp. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of burial mounds like the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}, {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Group|ja|古市古墳群}}, or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|Wa King San|ja|讃}}.

{{Cite sources clearly|date=September 2015}} '''Wa Zui''' (倭 隋, {{ill|Lifespan Unknown|ja|生没年不詳}}) was a figure from the early [[5th century]] (middle [[Kofun period]]) in [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]]. He served as a retainer of {{ill|Wa King Chin|ja|珍}}, one of the {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}.
== Records == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} According to Chinese historical texts such as the {{ill|Book of Song|ja|宋書}}, the {{ill|Wendi (Liu Song)|ja|文帝}} section, [[Yuanjia (Liu Song)|Yuanjia]] 15th year ({{ill|438|ja|438年}}) Jisi entry, and the section on foreign peoples in the {{ill|History of the Southern Dynasties|ja|南史}} (Chapter 69, "Eastern Barbarians of Wa")<ref name="Toyobunko">『{{ill|The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja|東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（東洋文庫264）}}』 Heibonsha, 1974, pp. 309-313.</ref><ref name="Kodansha">『{{ill|Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja|倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（講談社学術文庫2010）}}』 Kodansha, 2010, pp. 117-123.</ref>, Chin sent an envoy to the {{ill|Liu Song dynasty|ja|宋}} court, offering tributes and receiving the title of "General Pacifying the East and King of Wa." Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the {{ill|Yamato polity|ja|ヤマト王権}}.<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}. This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}} and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）}}』 Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2010, pp. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of burial mounds like the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}, {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Group|ja|古市古墳群}}, or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|Wa King San|ja|讃}}.
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Kawachi Haruhito|year=2018|title={{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja|倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-}} (Chūō Kōron Shinsho 2470)|publisher={{ill|Chūōkōron-shinsha|ja|中央公論新社}}|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|Kawachi Haruhito|2018}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}} * {{ill|Wa King Chin|ja|珍}}
== External Links == * [http://hanchi.ihp.sinica.edu.tw/ihp/hanji.htm Chinese Classics Electronic Database] - Academia Sinica, Taiwan

Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|Wa King San|ja|讃}}.

[[Category:Kofun period figures]] [[Category:Figures in Wa Chronicles]] [[Category:Date of birth unknown]] [[Category:Date of death unknown]] [[Category:Pages_with_redlinks_to_fill]][[Category:pp]][[category:Wa Sui]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Wa Sui]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Wa Sui]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Wa Sui]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Wa Sui]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:dardar]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Wa Sui]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:ファイル]] [[ja:倭隋]]

{{Cite sources clearly|date=September 2015}} '''Wa Zui''' (倭 隋, {{ill|Lifespan Unknown|ja|生没年不詳}}) was a figure from the early [[5th century]] (middle [[Kofun period]]) in [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]]. He served as a retainer of {{ill|Wa King Chin|ja|珍}}, one of the {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}.
== Records == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} According to Chinese historical texts such as the {{ill|Book of Song|ja|宋書}}, the {{ill|Wendi (Liu Song)|ja|文帝}} section, [[Yuanjia (Liu Song)|Yuanjia]] 15th year ({{ill|438|ja|438年}}) Jisi entry, and the section on foreign peoples in the {{ill|History of the Southern Dynasties|ja|南史}} (Chapter 69, "Eastern Barbarians of Wa")<ref name="Toyobunko">『{{ill|The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja|東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（東洋文庫264）}}』 Heibonsha, 1974, pp. 309-313.</ref><ref name="Kodansha">『{{ill|Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja|倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（講談社学術文庫2010）}}』 Kodansha, 2010, pp. 117-123.</ref>, Chin sent an envoy to the {{ill|Liu Song dynasty|ja|宋}} court, offering tributes and receiving the title of "General Pacifying the East and King of Wa." Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the {{ill|Yamato polity|ja|ヤマト王権}}.<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}. This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}} and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）}}』 Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2010, pp. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of burial mounds like the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}, {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Group|ja|古市古墳群}}, or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|Wa King San|ja|讃}}.
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Kawachi Haruhito|year=2018|title={{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja|倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-}} (Chūō Kōron Shinsho 2470)|publisher={{ill|Chūōkōron-shinsha|ja|中央公論新社}}|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|Kawachi Haruhito|2018}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}} * {{ill|Wa King Chin|ja|珍}}

== Records == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} According to Chinese historical texts such as the {{ill|Book of Song|ja|宋書}}, the {{ill|Wendi (Liu Song)|ja|文帝}} section, [[Yuanjia (Liu Song)|Yuanjia]] 15th year ({{ill|438|ja|438年}}) Jisi entry, and the section on foreign peoples in the {{ill|History of the Southern Dynasties|ja|南史}} (Chapter 69, "Eastern Barbarians of Wa")<ref name="Toyobunko">『{{ill|The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja|東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（東洋文庫264）}}』 Heibonsha, 1974, pp. 309-313.</ref><ref name="Kodansha">『{{ill|Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja|倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（講談社学術文庫2010）}}』 Kodansha, 2010, pp. 117-123.</ref>, Chin sent an envoy to the {{ill|Liu Song dynasty|ja|宋}} court, offering tributes and receiving the title of "General Pacifying the East and King of Wa." Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the {{ill|Yamato polity|ja|ヤマト王権}}.<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}. Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|San of Wa|ja|讃}}.

[[Category:Kofun period figures]] [[Category:Figures in Wa Chronicles]] [[Category:Date of birth unknown]] [[Category:Date of death unknown]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Wa Sui]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Wa Sui]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Wa Sui]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Wa Sui]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Wa Sui]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:dardar]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Wa Sui]][[category:original pages]] [[ja:倭隋]]

{{Cite sources clearly|date=September 2015}} '''Wa Zui''' (倭 隋, {{ill|Lifespan Unknown|ja|生没年不詳}}) was a figure from the early [[5th century]] (middle [[Kofun period]]) in [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]]. He served as a retainer of {{ill|Chin of Wa|ja|珍}}, one of the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}.
== Records == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} According to Chinese historical texts such as the {{ill|Book of Song|ja|宋書}}, the {{ill|Wendi (Liu Song)|ja|文帝}} section, [[Yuanjia (Liu Song)|Yuanjia]] 15th year ({{ill|438|ja|438年}}) Jisi entry, and the section on foreign peoples in the {{ill|History of the Southern Dynasties|ja|南史}} (Chapter 69, "Eastern Barbarians of Wa")<ref name="Toyobunko">『{{ill|The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja|東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（東洋文庫264）}}』 Heibonsha, 1974, pp. 309-313.</ref><ref name="Kodansha">『{{ill|Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja|倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（講談社学術文庫2010）}}』 Kodansha, 2010, pp. 117-123.</ref>, Chin sent an envoy to the {{ill|Liu Song dynasty|ja|宋}} court, offering tributes and receiving the title of "General Pacifying the East and King of Wa." Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|ヤマト王権}}.<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}. This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}} and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）}}』 Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2010, pp. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of burial mounds like the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}, {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Group|ja|古市古墳群}}, or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|San of Wa|ja|讃}}.
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Kawachi Haruhito|year=2018|title={{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja|倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-}} (Chūō Kōron Shinsho 2470)|publisher={{ill|Chūōkōron-shinsha|ja|中央公論新社}}|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|Kawachi Haruhito|2018}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}} * {{ill|Wa King Chin|ja|珍}}
== External Links == * [http://hanchi.ihp.sinica.edu.tw/ihp/hanji.htm Chinese Classics Electronic Database] - Academia Sinica, Taiwan

Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|San of Wa|ja|讃}}.

In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|San of Wa|ja|讃}}.

== References == * {{Cite book|author=Kawachi Haruhito|year=2018|title={{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja|倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-}} (Chūō Kōron Shinsho 2470)|publisher={{ill|Chūōkōron-shinsha|ja|中央公論新社}}|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|Kawachi Haruhito|2018}}}}

[[Category:Kofun period figures]] [[Category:Figures in Wa Chronicles]] [[Category:Date of birth unknown]] [[Category:Date of death unknown]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Wa Sui]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Wa Sui]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:Wa Sui]][[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Wa Sui]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:Wa Sui]][[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:Wa Sui]][[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Wa Sui]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:倭隋]]

{{Cite sources clearly|date=September 2015}} '''Wa Zui''' (倭 隋, {{ill|Lifespan Unknown|ja|生没年不詳}}) was a figure from the early [[5th century]] (middle [[Kofun period]]) in [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]]. He served as a retainer of {{ill|Chin of Wa|ja|珍}}, one of the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}.
== Records == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} According to Chinese historical texts such as the {{ill|Book of Song|ja|宋書}}, the {{ill|Wendi (Liu Song)|ja|文帝}} section, [[Yuanjia (Liu Song)|Yuanjia]] 15th year ({{ill|438|ja|438年}}) Jisi entry, and the section on foreign peoples in the {{ill|History of the Southern Dynasties|ja|南史}} (Chapter 69, "Eastern Barbarians of Wa")<ref name="Toyobunko">『{{ill|The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja|東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（東洋文庫264）}}』 Heibonsha, 1974, pp. 309-313.</ref><ref name="Kodansha">『{{ill|Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja|倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（講談社学術文庫2010）}}』 Kodansha, 2010, pp. 117-123.</ref>, Chin sent an envoy to the {{ill|Liu Song dynasty|ja|宋}} court, offering tributes and receiving the title of "General Pacifying the East and King of Wa." Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|ヤマト王権}}.<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}. This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}} and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）}}』 Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2010, pp. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of burial mounds like the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}, {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Group|ja|古市古墳群}}, or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|San of Wa|ja|讃}}.
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Kawachi Haruhito|year=2018|title={{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja|倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-}} (Chūō Kōron Shinsho 2470)|publisher={{ill|Chūōkōron-shinsha|ja|中央公論新社}}|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|Kawachi Haruhito|2018}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}} * {{ill|Wa King Chin|ja|珍}}
== External Links == * [http://hanchi.ihp.sinica.edu.tw/ihp/hanji.htm Chinese Classics Electronic Database] - Academia Sinica, Taiwan

This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}} and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）}}』 Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2010, pp. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of burial mounds like the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}, {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Group|ja|古市古墳群}}, or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|San of Wa|ja|讃}}.

{{Cite sources clearly|date=September 2015}} '''Wa Zui''' (倭 隋, {{ill|Lifespan Unknown|ja|生没年不詳}}) was a figure from the early [[5th century]] (middle [[Kofun period]]) in [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]]. He served as a retainer of {{ill|Chin of Wa|ja|珍}}, one of the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}.
== Records == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} According to Chinese historical texts such as the {{ill|Book of Song|ja|宋書}}, the {{ill|Wendi (Liu Song)|ja|文帝}} section, [[Yuanjia (Liu Song)|Yuanjia]] 15th year ({{ill|438|ja|438年}}) Jisi entry, and the section on foreign peoples in the {{ill|History of the Southern Dynasties|ja|南史}} (Chapter 69, "Eastern Barbarians of Wa")<ref name="Toyobunko">『{{ill|The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja|東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（東洋文庫264）}}』 Heibonsha, 1974, pp. 309-313.</ref><ref name="Kodansha">『{{ill|Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja|倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（講談社学術文庫2010）}}』 Kodansha, 2010, pp. 117-123.</ref>, Chin sent an envoy to the {{ill|Liu Song dynasty|ja|宋}} court, offering tributes and receiving the title of "General Pacifying the East and King of Wa." Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|ヤマト王権}}.<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}. This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}} and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）}}』 Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2010, pp. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of burial mounds like the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}, {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Group|ja|古市古墳群}}, or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|San of Wa|ja|讃}}.
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Kawachi Haruhito|year=2018|title={{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja|倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-}} (Chūō Kōron Shinsho 2470)|publisher={{ill|Chūōkōron-shinsha|ja|中央公論新社}}|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|Kawachi Haruhito|2018}}}}

In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|San of Wa|ja|讃}}.
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Kawachi Haruhito|year=2018|title={{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja|倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-}} (Chūō Kōron Shinsho 2470)|publisher={{ill|Chūōkōron-shinsha|ja|中央公論新社}}|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|Kawachi Haruhito|2018}}}}

revision.comment: 1 revision imported: attributing pages to myself
revision.text:
{{Cite sources clearly|date=September 2015}} '''Wa Zui''' (倭 隋, {{ill|Lifespan Unknown|ja|生没年不詳}}) was a figure from the early [[5th century]] (middle [[Kofun period]]) in [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]]. He served as a retainer of {{ill|Chin of Wa|ja|珍}}, one of the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}.
== Records == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} According to Chinese historical texts such as the {{ill|Book of Song|ja|宋書}}, the {{ill|Wendi (Liu Song)|ja|文帝}} section, [[Yuanjia (Liu Song)|Yuanjia]] 15th year ({{ill|438|ja|438年}}) Jisi entry, and the section on foreign peoples in the {{ill|History of the Southern Dynasties|ja|南史}} (Chapter 69, "Eastern Barbarians of Wa")<ref name="Toyobunko">『{{ill|The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja|東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（東洋文庫264）}}』 Heibonsha, 1974, pp. 309-313.</ref><ref name="Kodansha">『{{ill|Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja|倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（講談社学術文庫2010）}}』 Kodansha, 2010, pp. 117-123.</ref>, Chin sent an envoy to the {{ill|Liu Song dynasty|ja|宋}} court, offering tributes and receiving the title of "General Pacifying the East and King of Wa." Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|ヤマト王権}}.<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}. This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}} and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）}}』 Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2010, pp. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of burial mounds like the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}, {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Group|ja|古市古墳群}}, or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|San of Wa|ja|讃}}.
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Kawachi Haruhito|year=2018|title={{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja|倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-}} (Chūō Kōron Shinsho 2470)|publisher={{ill|Chūōkōron-shinsha|ja|中央公論新社}}|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|Kawachi Haruhito|2018}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Five Kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}} * {{ill|Wa King Chin|ja|珍}}
== External Links == * [http://hanchi.ihp.sinica.edu.tw/ihp/hanji.htm Chinese Classics Electronic Database] - Academia Sinica, Taiwan

{{Cite sources clearly|date=September 2015}} '''Wa Zui''' (倭 隋, {{ill|Lifespan Unknown|ja|生没年不詳}}) was a figure from the early [[5th century]] (middle [[Kofun period]]) in [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]]. He served as a retainer of {{ill|Chin of Wa|ja|珍}}, one of the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}.
== Records == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} According to Chinese historical texts such as the {{ill|Book of Song|ja|宋書}}, the {{ill|Wendi (Liu Song)|ja|文帝}} section, [[Yuanjia (Liu Song)|Yuanjia]] 15th year ({{ill|438|ja|438年}}) Jisi entry, and the section on foreign peoples in the {{ill|History of the Southern Dynasties|ja|南史}} (Chapter 69, "Eastern Barbarians of Wa")<ref name="Toyobunko">『{{ill|The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja|東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（東洋文庫264）}}』 Heibonsha, 1974, pp. 309-313.</ref><ref name="Kodansha">『{{ill|Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja|倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（講談社学術文庫2010）}}』 Kodansha, 2010, pp. 117-123.</ref>, Chin sent an envoy to the {{ill|Liu Song dynasty|ja|宋}} court, offering tributes and receiving the title of "General Pacifying the East and King of Wa." Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|ヤマト王権}}.<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}. This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}} and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）}}』 Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2010, pp. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of burial mounds like the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}, {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Group|ja|古市古墳群}}, or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|San of Wa|ja|讃}}.
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Kawachi Haruhito|year=2018|title={{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja|倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-}} (Chūō Kōron Shinsho 2470)|publisher={{ill|Chūōkōron-shinsha|ja|中央公論新社}}|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|Kawachi Haruhito|2018}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}} * {{ill|Chin of Wa|ja|珍}}

This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.

Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|ヤマト王権}}.<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}. This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}} and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）}}』 Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2010, pp. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of burial mounds like the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}, {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Group|ja|古市古墳群}}, or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|San of Wa|ja|讃}}.

{{Cite sources clearly|date=September 2015}} '''Wa Zui''' (倭 隋, {{ill|Lifespan Unknown|ja|生没年不詳}}) was a figure from the early [[5th century]] (middle [[Kofun period]]) in [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]]. He served as a retainer of {{ill|Chin of Wa|ja|珍}}, one of the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}.
== Records == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} According to Chinese historical texts such as the {{ill|Book of Song|ja|宋書}}, the {{ill|Wendi (Liu Song)|ja|文帝}} section, [[Yuanjia (Liu Song)|Yuanjia]] 15th year ({{ill|438|ja|438年}}) Jisi entry, and the section on foreign peoples in the {{ill|History of the Southern Dynasties|ja|南史}} (Chapter 69, "Eastern Barbarians of Wa")<ref name="Toyobunko">『{{ill|The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja|東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（東洋文庫264）}}』 Heibonsha, 1974, pp. 309-313.</ref><ref name="Kodansha">『{{ill|Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja|倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（講談社学術文庫2010）}}』 Kodansha, 2010, pp. 117-123.</ref>, Chin sent an envoy to the {{ill|Liu Song dynasty|ja|宋}} court, offering tributes and receiving the title of "General Pacifying the East and King of Wa." Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|ヤマト王権}}.<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}. This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}} and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）}}』 Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2010, pp. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of burial mounds like the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}, {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Group|ja|古市古墳群}}, or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|San of Wa|ja|讃}}.
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Kawachi Haruhito|year=2018|title={{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja|倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-}} (Chūō Kōron Shinsho 2470)|publisher={{ill|Chūōkōron-shinsha|ja|中央公論新社}}|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|Kawachi Haruhito|2018}}}}

This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|San of Wa|ja|讃}}.

{{Cite sources clearly|date=September 2015}} '''Wa Zui''' (倭 隋, {{ill|Lifespan Unknown|ja|生没年不詳}}) was a figure from the early [[5th century]] (middle [[Kofun period]]) in [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]]. He served as a retainer of {{ill|Chin of Wa|ja|珍}}, one of the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}.
== Records == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} According to Chinese historical texts such as the {{ill|Book of Song|ja|宋書}}, the {{ill|Wendi (Liu Song)|ja|文帝}} section, [[Yuanjia (Liu Song)|Yuanjia]] 15th year ({{ill|438|ja|438年}}) Jisi entry, and the section on foreign peoples in the {{ill|History of the Southern Dynasties|ja|南史}} (Chapter 69, "Eastern Barbarians of Wa")<ref name="Toyobunko">『{{ill|The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja|東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（東洋文庫264）}}』 Heibonsha, 1974, pp. 309-313.</ref><ref name="Kodansha">『{{ill|Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja|倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（講談社学術文庫2010）}}』 Kodansha, 2010, pp. 117-123.</ref>, Chin sent an envoy to the {{ill|Liu Song dynasty|ja|宋}} court, offering tributes and receiving the title of "General Pacifying the East and King of Wa." Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|ヤマト王権}}.<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}. This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}} and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）}}』 Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2010, pp. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of burial mounds like the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}, {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Group|ja|古市古墳群}}, or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|San of Wa|ja|讃}}.

One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|San of Wa|ja|讃}}.

{{Cite sources clearly|date=September 2015}} '''Wa Zui''' (倭 隋, {{ill|Lifespan Unknown|ja|生没年不詳}}) was a figure from the early [[5th century]] (middle [[Kofun period]]) in [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]]. He served as a retainer of {{ill|Chin of Wa|ja|珍}}, one of the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}.
== Records == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} According to Chinese historical texts such as the {{ill|Book of Song|ja|宋書}}, the {{ill|Wendi (Liu Song)|ja|文帝}} section, [[Yuanjia (Liu Song)|Yuanjia]] 15th year ({{ill|438|ja|438年}}) Jisi entry, and the section on foreign peoples in the {{ill|History of the Southern Dynasties|ja|南史}} (Chapter 69, "Eastern Barbarians of Wa")<ref name="Toyobunko">『{{ill|The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja|東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（東洋文庫264）}}』 Heibonsha, 1974, pp. 309-313.</ref><ref name="Kodansha">『{{ill|Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja|倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（講談社学術文庫2010）}}』 Kodansha, 2010, pp. 117-123.</ref>, Chin sent an envoy to the {{ill|Liu Song dynasty|ja|宋}} court, offering tributes and receiving the title of "General Pacifying the East and King of Wa." Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|ヤマト王権}}.<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}. This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}} and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）}}』 Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2010, pp. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of burial mounds like the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}, {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Group|ja|古市古墳群}}, or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|San of Wa|ja|讃}}.

== References == * {{Cite book|author=Kawachi Haruhito|year=2018|title={{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja|倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-}} (Chūō Kōron Shinsho 2470)|publisher={{ill|Chūōkōron-shinsha|ja|中央公論新社}}|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|Kawachi Haruhito|2018}}}}

{{Cite sources clearly|date=September 2015}} '''Wa Zui''' (倭 隋, {{ill|Lifespan Unknown|ja|生没年不詳}}) was a figure from the early [[5th century]] (middle [[Kofun period]]) in [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]]. He served as a retainer of {{ill|Chin of Wa|ja|珍}}, one of the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}.
== Records == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} According to Chinese historical texts such as the {{ill|Book of Song|ja|宋書}}, the {{ill|Wendi (Liu Song)|ja|文帝}} section, [[Yuanjia (Liu Song)|Yuanjia]] 15th year ({{ill|438|ja|438年}}) Jisi entry, and the section on foreign peoples in the {{ill|History of the Southern Dynasties|ja|南史}} (Chapter 69, "Eastern Barbarians of Wa")<ref name="Toyobunko">『{{ill|The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja|東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（東洋文庫264）}}』 Heibonsha, 1974, pp. 309-313.</ref><ref name="Kodansha">『{{ill|Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja|倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（講談社学術文庫2010）}}』 Kodansha, 2010, pp. 117-123.</ref>, Chin sent an envoy to the {{ill|Liu Song dynasty|ja|宋}} court, offering tributes and receiving the title of "General Pacifying the East and King of Wa." Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|ヤマト王権}}.<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}. This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}} and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）}}』 Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2010, pp. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of burial mounds like the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}, {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Group|ja|古市古墳群}}, or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|San of Wa|ja|讃}}.
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Kawachi Haruhito|year=2018|title={{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja|倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-}} (Chūō Kōron Shinsho 2470)|publisher={{ill|Chūōkōron-shinsha|ja|中央公論新社}}|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|Kawachi Haruhito|2018}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}} * {{ill|Chin of Wa|ja|珍}}
== External Links == * [http://hanchi.ihp.sinica.edu.tw/ihp/hanji.htm Chinese Classics Electronic Database] - Academia Sinica, Taiwan

Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|pp=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達}}, a retainer of {{ill|San of Wa|ja|讃}}.
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Kawachi Haruhito|year=2018|title={{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja|倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-}} (Chūō Kōron Shinsho 2470)|publisher={{ill|Chūōkōron-shinsha|ja|中央公論新社}}|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|Kawachi Haruhito|2018}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Five kings of Wa|ja|倭の五王}} * {{ill|Chin of Wa|ja|珍}}
== External Links == * [http://hanchi.ihp.sinica.edu.tw/ihp/hanji.htm Chinese Classics Electronic Database] - Academia Sinica, Taiwan
