<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:倭隋]][[zh:倭隋]] {{Infobox person|nationality={{ill|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424}}|given_name={{ill|Sui|WD=Q110880075}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q55523253]] Wikidata name: Wa Zui [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced clearly|date=September 2015}} '''Wa Zui''' (倭 隋, {{ill|Lifespan Unknown|ja|生没年不詳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lifespan Unknown|1=unknown vital dates|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574588}}) was a figure from the early [[:en:5th century|5th century]] (middle [[Kofun period]]) in [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]. He served as a retainer of [[:en:Chin of Wa|Chin of Wa]], one of the [[:en:Five kings of Wa|Five kings of Wa]].
== Records == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} According to Chinese historical texts such as the [[:en:Book of Song|Book of Song]], the [[:en:Emperor Wen|Wendi (Liu Song)]] section, [[Yuanjia (Liu Song)|Yuanjia]] 15th year ([[:en:438|438]]) Jisi entry, and the section on foreign peoples in the [[:en:History of the Southern Dynasties|History of the Southern Dynasties]] (Chapter 69, "Eastern Barbarians of Wa")<ref name="Toyobunko">『{{ill|The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja|東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（東洋文庫264）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Heibonsha, 1974, 11. 309-313.</ref><ref name="Kodansha">『{{ill|Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja|倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（講談社学術文庫2010）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Kodansha, 2010, 11. 117-123.</ref>, Chin sent an envoy to the {{ill|Liu Song dynasty|ja|宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Liu Song dynasty|1=Song dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10831703}} court, offering tributes and receiving the title of "General Pacifying the East and King of Wa." Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato kingship]].<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the Rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third Rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}. This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between [[:en:Himiko|Himiko]] and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2010, 11. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of Kofuns like the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Tombs]], [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Group]], or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}.
Regarding the [[:en:Five kings of Wa|Five kings of Wa]], if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the [[:en:Five kings of Wa|Five kings of Wa]], the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sima Cao Da|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ソーダ|1=Soda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2027844}}, a retainer of [[:en:San of Wa|San of Wa]].
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Kawachi Haruhito|year=2018|title={{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja|倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Chūō Kōron Shinsho 2470)|publisher=[[:en:Chuokoron-Shinsha|Chūōkōron-shinsha]]|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|Kawachi Haruhito|2018}}}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:倭隋]][[zh:倭隋]] {{Infobox person|nationality={{ill|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424}}|given_name={{ill|Sui|WD=Q110880075}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q55523253]] Wikidata name: Wa Zui [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced clearly|date=September 2015}} '''Wa Zui''' (倭 隋, {{ill|Lifespan Unknown|ja|生没年不詳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lifespan Unknown|1=unknown vital dates|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574588}}) was a figure from the early [[:en:5th century|5th century]] (middle [[Kofun period]]) in [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]. He served as a retainer of [[:en:Chin of Wa|Chin of Wa]], one of the [[:en:Five kings of Wa|Five kings of Wa]].
== Records == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} According to Chinese historical texts such as the [[:en:Book of Song|Book of Song]], the [[:en:Emperor Wen|Wendi (Liu Song)]] section, [[Yuanjia (Liu Song)|Yuanjia]] 15th year ([[:en:438|438]]) Jisi entry, and the section on foreign peoples in the [[:en:History of the Southern Dynasties|History of the Southern Dynasties]] (Chapter 69, "Eastern Barbarians of Wa")<ref name="Toyobunko">『{{ill|The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja|東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（東洋文庫264）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Heibonsha, 1974, 11. 309-313.</ref><ref name="Kodansha">『{{ill|Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja|倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（講談社学術文庫2010）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Kodansha, 2010, 11. 117-123.</ref>, Chin sent an envoy to the {{ill|Liu Song dynasty|ja|宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Liu Song dynasty|1=Song dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10831703}} court, offering tributes and receiving the title of "General Pacifying the East and King of Wa." Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato kingship]].<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the Rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third Rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}. This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between [[:en:Himiko|Himiko]] and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2010, 11. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of Kofuns like the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Tombs]], [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Group]], or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}.
Regarding the [[:en:Five kings of Wa|Five kings of Wa]], if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the [[:en:Five kings of Wa|Five kings of Wa]], the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sima Cao Da|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ソーダ|1=Soda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2027844}}, a retainer of [[:en:San of Wa|San of Wa]].
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Kawachi Haruhito|year=2018|title={{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja|倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Chūō Kōron Shinsho 2470)|publisher=[[:en:Chuokoron-Shinsha|Chūōkōron-shinsha]]|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|Kawachi Haruhito|2018}}}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Five kings of Wa|Five kings of Wa]] * [[:en:Chin of Wa|Chin of Wa]]

These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato kingship]].<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the Rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third Rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}. This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between [[:en:Himiko|Himiko]] and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2010, 11. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of Kofuns like the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Tombs]], [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Group]], or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}.
Regarding the [[:en:Five kings of Wa|Five kings of Wa]], if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the [[:en:Five kings of Wa|Five kings of Wa]], the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sima Cao Da|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ソーダ|1=Soda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2027844}}, a retainer of [[:en:San of Wa|San of Wa]].
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Kawachi Haruhito|year=2018|title={{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja|倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Chūō Kōron Shinsho 2470)|publisher=[[:en:Chuokoron-Shinsha|Chūōkōron-shinsha]]|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|Kawachi Haruhito|2018}}}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Five kings of Wa|Five kings of Wa]] * [[:en:Chin of Wa|Chin of Wa]]
== External Links == * [http://hanchi.ihp.sinica.edu.tw/ihp/hanji.htm Chinese Classics Electronic Database] - Academia Sinica, Taiwan

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:倭隋]][[zh:倭隋]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:倭隋]][[zh:倭隋]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:倭隋]][[zh:倭隋]] {{Infobox person|nationality={{ill|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424}}|given_name={{ill|Sui|WD=Q110880075}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q55523253]] Wikidata name: Wa Zui [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced clearly|date=September 2015}} '''Wa Zui''' (倭 隋, {{ill|Lifespan Unknown|ja|生没年不詳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lifespan Unknown|1=unknown vital dates|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574588}}) was a figure from the early [[:en:5th century|5th century]] (middle [[Kofun period]]) in [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]. He served as a retainer of [[:en:Chin of Wa|Chin of Wa]], one of the [[:en:Five kings of Wa|Five kings of Wa]].
== Records == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} According to Chinese historical texts such as the [[:en:Book of Song|Book of Song]], the [[:en:Emperor Wen|Wendi (Liu Song)]] section, [[Yuanjia (Liu Song)|Yuanjia]] 15th year ([[:en:438|438]]) Jisi entry, and the section on foreign peoples in the [[:en:History of the Southern Dynasties|History of the Southern Dynasties]] (Chapter 69, "Eastern Barbarians of Wa")<ref name="Toyobunko">『{{ill|The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja|東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（東洋文庫264）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Heibonsha, 1974, 11. 309-313.</ref><ref name="Kodansha">『{{ill|Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja|倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（講談社学術文庫2010）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Kodansha, 2010, 11. 117-123.</ref>, Chin sent an envoy to the {{ill|Liu Song dynasty|ja|宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Liu Song dynasty|1=Song dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10831703}} court, offering tributes and receiving the title of "General Pacifying the East and King of Wa." Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato kingship]].<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the Rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third Rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}. This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between [[:en:Himiko|Himiko]] and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2010, 11. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of Kofuns like the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Tombs]], [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Group]], or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}.
Regarding the [[:en:Five kings of Wa|Five kings of Wa]], if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the [[:en:Five kings of Wa|Five kings of Wa]], the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sima Cao Da|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ソーダ|1=Soda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2027844}}, a retainer of [[:en:San of Wa|San of Wa]].
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Kawachi Haruhito|year=2018|title={{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja|倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Chūō Kōron Shinsho 2470)|publisher=[[:en:Chuokoron-Shinsha|Chūōkōron-shinsha]]|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|Kawachi Haruhito|2018}}}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:倭隋]][[zh:倭隋]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:倭隋]][[zh:倭隋]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:倭隋]][[zh:倭隋]] {{Infobox person|nationality={{ill|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424}}|given_name={{ill|Sui|WD=Q110880075}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q55523253]] Wikidata name: Wa Zui [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced clearly|date=September 2015}} '''Wa Zui''' (倭 隋, {{ill|Lifespan Unknown|ja|生没年不詳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lifespan Unknown|1=unknown vital dates|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574588}}) was a figure from the early [[:en:5th century|5th century]] (middle [[Kofun period]]) in [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]. He served as a retainer of [[:en:Chin of Wa|Chin of Wa]], one of the [[:en:Five kings of Wa|Five kings of Wa]].
== Records == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} According to Chinese historical texts such as the [[:en:Book of Song|Book of Song]], the [[:en:Emperor Wen|Wendi (Liu Song)]] section, [[Yuanjia (Liu Song)|Yuanjia]] 15th year ([[:en:438|438]]) Jisi entry, and the section on foreign peoples in the [[:en:History of the Southern Dynasties|History of the Southern Dynasties]] (Chapter 69, "Eastern Barbarians of Wa")<ref name="Toyobunko">『{{ill|The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja|東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（東洋文庫264）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Heibonsha, 1974, 11. 309-313.</ref><ref name="Kodansha">『{{ill|Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja|倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（講談社学術文庫2010）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Kodansha, 2010, 11. 117-123.</ref>, Chin sent an envoy to the {{ill|Liu Song dynasty|ja|宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Liu Song dynasty|1=Song dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10831703}} court, offering tributes and receiving the title of "General Pacifying the East and King of Wa." Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato kingship]].<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the Rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third Rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}. This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between [[:en:Himiko|Himiko]] and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2010, 11. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of Kofuns like the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Tombs]], [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Group]], or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}.
Regarding the [[:en:Five kings of Wa|Five kings of Wa]], if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the [[:en:Five kings of Wa|Five kings of Wa]], the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sima Cao Da|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ソーダ|1=Soda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2027844}}, a retainer of [[:en:San of Wa|San of Wa]].
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Kawachi Haruhito|year=2018|title={{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja|倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Chūō Kōron Shinsho 2470)|publisher=[[:en:Chuokoron-Shinsha|Chūōkōron-shinsha]]|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|Kawachi Haruhito|2018}}}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Five kings of Wa|Five kings of Wa]] * [[:en:Chin of Wa|Chin of Wa]]
== External Links == * [http://hanchi.ihp.sinica.edu.tw/ihp/hanji.htm Chinese Classics Electronic Database] - Academia Sinica, Taiwan
{{translated page|ja|倭隋|version=98772583|comment=Imported full ja history}}

This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between {{ill|Himiko|en|Himiko|WD=Q234451|lt=Himiko}} and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2010, 11. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of Kofuns like the {{ill|Mozu Tombs|en|Mozu Tombs|WD=Q4250584|lt=Mozu Tombs}}, {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|en|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|WD=Q4250076|lt=Furuichi Kofun Group}}, or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}.

This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|en|Five kings of Wa|WD=Q1152880|lt=Five kings of Wa}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:倭隋]][[zh:倭隋]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:倭隋]][[zh:倭隋]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:倭隋]][[zh:倭隋]] {{Infobox person|nationality={{ill|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424}}|given_name={{ill|Sui|WD=Q110880075}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q55523253]] Wikidata name: Wa Zui [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced clearly|date=September 2015}} '''Wa Zui''' (倭 隋, {{ill|Lifespan Unknown|ja|生没年不詳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lifespan Unknown|1=unknown vital dates|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574588}}) was a figure from the early {{ill|5th century|en|5th century|WD=Q8095|lt=5th century}} (middle [[Kofun period]]) in [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]. He served as a retainer of {{ill|Chin of Wa|en|Chin of Wa|WD=Q28692298|lt=Chin of Wa}}, one of the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|en|Five kings of Wa|WD=Q1152880|lt=Five kings of Wa}}.
== Records == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} According to Chinese historical texts such as the {{ill|Book of Song|en|Book of Song|WD=Q1143142|lt=Book of Song}}, the {{ill|Emperor Wen|en|Emperor Wen|WD=Q671817|lt=Wendi (Liu Song)}} section, [[Yuanjia (Liu Song)|Yuanjia]] 15th year ({{ill|438|en|438|WD=Q33548|lt=438}}) Jisi entry, and the section on foreign peoples in the {{ill|History of the Southern Dynasties|en|History of the Southern Dynasties|WD=Q1137686|lt=History of the Southern Dynasties}} (Chapter 69, "Eastern Barbarians of Wa")<ref name="Toyobunko">『{{ill|The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja|東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（東洋文庫264）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Heibonsha, 1974, 11. 309-313.</ref><ref name="Kodansha">『{{ill|Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja|倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（講談社学術文庫2010）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Kodansha, 2010, 11. 117-123.</ref>, Chin sent an envoy to the {{ill|Liu Song dynasty|ja|宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Liu Song dynasty|1=Song dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10831703}} court, offering tributes and receiving the title of "General Pacifying the East and King of Wa." Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato kingship}}.<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the Rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third Rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}. This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between {{ill|Himiko|en|Himiko|WD=Q234451|lt=Himiko}} and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2010, 11. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of Kofuns like the {{ill|Mozu Tombs|en|Mozu Tombs|WD=Q4250584|lt=Mozu Tombs}}, {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|en|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|WD=Q4250076|lt=Furuichi Kofun Group}}, or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|en|Five kings of Wa|WD=Q1152880|lt=Five kings of Wa}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|en|Five kings of Wa|WD=Q1152880|lt=Five kings of Wa}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sima Cao Da|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ソーダ|1=Soda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2027844}}, a retainer of {{ill|San of Wa|en|San of Wa|WD=Q28692297|lt=San of Wa}}.
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Kawachi Haruhito|year=2018|title={{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja|倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Chūō Kōron Shinsho 2470)|publisher={{ill|Chuokoron-Shinsha|en|Chuokoron-Shinsha|WD=Q996095|lt=Chūōkōron-shinsha}}|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|Kawachi Haruhito|2018}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Five kings of Wa|en|Five kings of Wa|WD=Q1152880|lt=Five kings of Wa}} * {{ill|Chin of Wa|en|Chin of Wa|WD=Q28692298|lt=Chin of Wa}}
== External Links == * [http://hanchi.ihp.sinica.edu.tw/ihp/hanji.htm Chinese Classics Electronic Database] - Academia Sinica, Taiwan
{{translated page|ja|倭隋|version=98772583|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:倭隋]][[zh:倭隋]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:倭隋]][[zh:倭隋]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:倭隋]][[zh:倭隋]] {{Infobox person|nationality={{ill|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|1=Wakoku}}|given_name={{ill|Sui|WD=Q110880075|1=Sui}}}} {{Unreferenced clearly|date=September 2015}} '''Wa Zui''' (倭 隋, {{ill|Lifespan Unknown|ja|生没年不詳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lifespan Unknown|1=unknown vital dates|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574588}}) was a figure from the early {{ill|5th century|en|5th century|WD=Q8095|lt=5th century|1=5th century}} (middle [[Kofun period]]) in [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]. He served as a retainer of {{ill|Chin of Wa|en|Chin of Wa|WD=Q28692298|lt=Chin of Wa|1=Chin of Wa}}, one of the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|en|Five kings of Wa|WD=Q1152880|lt=Five kings of Wa|1=Five kings of Wa}}.
== Records == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} According to Chinese historical texts such as the {{ill|Book of Song|en|Book of Song|WD=Q1143142|lt=Book of Song|1=Book of Song}}, the {{ill|Emperor Wen|en|Emperor Wen|WD=Q671817|lt=Wendi (Liu Song)|1=Emperor Wen}} section, [[Yuanjia (Liu Song)|Yuanjia]] 15th year ({{ill|438|en|438|WD=Q33548|lt=438|1=438}}) Jisi entry, and the section on foreign peoples in the {{ill|History of the Southern Dynasties|en|History of the Southern Dynasties|WD=Q1137686|lt=History of the Southern Dynasties|1=History of the Southern Dynasties}} (Chapter 69, "Eastern Barbarians of Wa")<ref name="Toyobunko">『{{ill|The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja|東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（東洋文庫264）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Heibonsha, 1974, 11. 309-313.</ref><ref name="Kodansha">『{{ill|Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja|倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（講談社学術文庫2010）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Kodansha, 2010, 11. 117-123.</ref>, Chin sent an envoy to the {{ill|Liu Song dynasty|ja|宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Liu Song dynasty|1=Song dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10831703}} court, offering tributes and receiving the title of "General Pacifying the East and King of Wa." Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}}.<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=National History Encyclopedia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the Rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third Rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}. This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between {{ill|Himiko|en|Himiko|WD=Q234451|lt=Himiko|1=Himiko}} and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2010, 11. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of Kofuns like the {{ill|Mozu Tombs|en|Mozu Tombs|WD=Q4250584|lt=Mozu Tombs|1=Mozu Tombs}}, {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|en|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|WD=Q4250076|lt=Furuichi Kofun Group|1=Furuichi Kofun Cluster}}, or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|en|Five kings of Wa|WD=Q1152880|lt=Five kings of Wa|1=Five kings of Wa}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|en|Five kings of Wa|WD=Q1152880|lt=Five kings of Wa|1=Five kings of Wa}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sima Cao Da|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ソーダ|1=Soda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2027844}}, a retainer of {{ill|San of Wa|en|San of Wa|WD=Q28692297|lt=San of Wa|1=San of Wa}}.
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Kawachi Haruhito|year=2018|title={{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja|倭の五王 -王位継承と五世紀の東アジア-|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Five Kings of Wa: Succession and 5th-Century East Asia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Chūō Kōron Shinsho 2470)|publisher={{ill|Chuokoron-Shinsha|en|Chuokoron-Shinsha|WD=Q996095|lt=Chūōkōron-shinsha|1=Chuokoron-Shinsha}}|isbn=978-4121024701|ref={{Harvid|Kawachi Haruhito|2018}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Five kings of Wa|en|Five kings of Wa|WD=Q1152880|lt=Five kings of Wa|1=Five kings of Wa}} * {{ill|Chin of Wa|en|Chin of Wa|WD=Q28692298|lt=Chin of Wa|1=Chin of Wa}}
== External Links == * [http://hanchi.ihp.sinica.edu.tw/ihp/hanji.htm Chinese Classics Electronic Database] - Academia Sinica, Taiwan

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:倭隋]][[zh:倭隋]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:倭隋]][[zh:倭隋]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:倭隋]][[zh:倭隋]] {{Infobox person|nationality={{ill|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|1=Wakoku}}|given_name={{ill|Sui|WD=Q110880075|1=Sui}}}} {{Unreferenced clearly|date=September 2015}} '''Wa Zui''' (倭 隋, {{ill|Lifespan Unknown|ja|生没年不詳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lifespan Unknown|1=unknown vital dates|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574588}}) was a figure from the early {{ill|5th century|en|5th century|WD=Q8095|lt=5th century|1=5th century}} (middle [[Kofun period]]) in [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]. He served as a retainer of {{ill|Chin of Wa|en|Chin of Wa|WD=Q28692298|lt=Chin of Wa|1=Chin of Wa}}, one of the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|en|Five kings of Wa|WD=Q1152880|lt=Five kings of Wa|1=Five kings of Wa}}.
== Records == {{Wikisource|宋書倭国伝}} According to Chinese historical texts such as the {{ill|Book of Song|en|Book of Song|WD=Q1143142|lt=Book of Song|1=Book of Song}}, the {{ill|Emperor Wen|en|Emperor Wen|WD=Q671817|lt=Wendi (Liu Song)|1=Emperor Wen}} section, [[Yuanjia (Liu Song)|Yuanjia]] 15th year ({{ill|438|en|438|WD=Q33548|lt=438|1=438}}) Jisi entry, and the section on foreign peoples in the {{ill|History of the Southern Dynasties|en|History of the Southern Dynasties|WD=Q1137686|lt=History of the Southern Dynasties|1=History of the Southern Dynasties}} (Chapter 69, "Eastern Barbarians of Wa")<ref name="Toyobunko">『{{ill|The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja|東アジア民族史 1 正史東夷伝（東洋文庫264）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Ethnic History of East Asia 1: Historical Records on Eastern Barbarians (Toyobunko 264)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Heibonsha, 1974, 11. 309-313.</ref><ref name="Kodansha">『{{ill|Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja|倭国伝 中国正史に描かれた日本（講談社学術文庫2010）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Records of Wa in Chinese Official Histories (Kodansha Academic Library 2010)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Kodansha, 2010, 11. 117-123.</ref>, Chin sent an envoy to the {{ill|Liu Song dynasty|ja|宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Liu Song dynasty|1=Song dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10831703}} court, offering tributes and receiving the title of "General Pacifying the East and King of Wa." Chin also requested military titles for Wa Zui and 12 others, including General Pacifying the West, General Campaigning Against Rebels, General Champion, and General Assisting the Nation. These requests were granted by Emperor Wendi through an imperial edict.
== Analysis == Details about Wa Zui remain unclear, but the shared surname "Wa" with the Wa King suggests that royal family generals were part of the ruling power structure within the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}}.<ref>Sakamoto Yoshitane, "The Five Kings of Wa," in 『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=National History Encyclopedia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}』, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan.</ref> Notably, the Rank of General Pacifying the West, similar to General Pacifying the East, only differs by one tier within the third Rank, indicating a hierarchical but parallel relationship<ref name="Mori"/>{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}. This has led to theories that Wa governance involved co-rule by figures of equal standing, akin to the relationship between {{ill|Himiko|en|Himiko|WD=Q234451|lt=Himiko|1=Himiko}} and her brother, reflecting a shared governance model.<ref name="Mori">Mori Kimikazu, 『{{ill|The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja|倭の五王 5世紀の東アジアと倭王群像（日本史リブレット 人 002）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Five Kings of Wa: 5th-Century East Asia and the Wa Kings|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2010, 11. 47-50.</ref> This governance model is also compared to the coexistence of Kofuns like the {{ill|Mozu Tombs|en|Mozu Tombs|WD=Q4250584|lt=Mozu Tombs|1=Mozu Tombs}}, {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|en|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|WD=Q4250076|lt=Furuichi Kofun Group|1=Furuichi Kofun Cluster}}, or {{ill|Saki Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}.
Additionally, while titles like "General Campaigning Against the East" or "General Pacifying the East" reflect a Chinese perspective, Wa Zui's title of "General Pacifying the West" indicates a Wa-centric view. This has led to theories proposing a governance structure with two central figures—General Pacifying the East and General Pacifying the West—overseeing generals like General Campaigning Against Rebels and General Assisting the Nation{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}.
Regarding the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|en|Five kings of Wa|WD=Q1152880|lt=Five kings of Wa|1=Five kings of Wa}}, if Chin and Sai are considered separate individuals, it remains unclear how they are related. One theory posits that they represented separate power groups (e.g., Mozu and Furuichi Kofun Groups) and that Sai may have been connected to Wa Zui’s lineage{{Sfn|Kawachi Haruhito|2018|11=73-119}}.
In the recorded articles about the {{ill|Five kings of Wa|en|Five kings of Wa|WD=Q1152880|lt=Five kings of Wa|1=Five kings of Wa}}, the only named individuals besides the kings themselves are Wa Zui and {{ill|Sima Cao Da|ja|曹達|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sima Cao Da|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ソーダ|1=Soda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2027844}}, a retainer of {{ill|San of Wa|en|San of Wa|WD=Q28692297|lt=San of Wa|1=San of Wa}}.
